title: Charmed Life — 1 (Fragment 3)
sourceText:
Accordingly, Mrs. Sharp turned the box out onto the table. It was a queer collection of things—letters and lace and souvenirs. Cat did not remember having seen half of them before. There was a marriage certificate, saying that Francis John Chant had married Caroline Mary Chant twelve years ago at St. Margaret’s Church, Wolvercote, and a withered nosegay his mother must have carried at the wedding. Underneath that, he found some glittery earrings he had never seen his mother wear.

Mrs. Sharp’s hat rattled as she bent swiftly over these. “Those are diamond earrings!” she said. “Your ma must have had money! Now, if I took those to Mr. Nostrum—But we’d get more for them if I took them around to Mr. Larkins.” Mr. Larkins kept the junk shop on the corner of the street—except that it was not always exactly junk. Among the brass fenders and chipped crockery you could find quite valuable things, and also a discreet notice saying Exotic Supplies —which meant that Mr. Larkins also stocked bats’ wings, dried newts, and other ingredients of magic. There was no question that Mr. Larkins would be very interested in a pair of diamond earrings. Mrs. Sharp’s eyes pouched up, greedy and beady, as she put out her hand to pick up the earrings.

Gwendolen put out her hand for them at the same moment. She did not say anything. Neither did Mrs. Sharp. Both their hands stood still in the air. There was a feeling of fierce invisible struggle. Then Mrs. Sharp took her hand away. “Thank you,” Gwendolen said coldly, and put the earrings away in the pocket of her black dress.

“You see what I mean?” Mrs. Sharp said, making the best of it. “You have real talent, dearie!” She went back to sorting the other things in the box. She turned over an old pipe, ribbons, a spray of white heather, menus, concert tickets, and picked up a bundle of old letters. She ran her thumb down the edge of it. “Love letters,” she said. “His to her.” She put the bundle down without looking at it and picked up another. “Hers to him. No use.” Cat, watching Mrs. Sharp’s broad mauve thumb whirring down a third bundle of letters, thought that being a witch must save a great deal of time. “Business letters,” said Mrs. Sharp. Her thumb paused, and went slowly back up the pile again. “Now what have we here?” she said. She untied the pink tape from around the bundle and carefully took out three letters. She unfolded them.

“Chrestomanci!” she exclaimed. And, as soon as she said it, she clapped one hand over her mouth and mumbled behind it. Her face was red. Cat could see she was surprised, frightened, and greedy, all at the same time. “Now what was he doing writing to your pa?” she said, as soon as she had recovered.

“Let’s see,” said Gwendolen.

Mrs. Sharp spread the three letters out on the kitchen table, and Gwendolen and Cat bent over them. The first thing that struck Cat was the energy of the signature on all three:

words.definition: As a result or consequence of something specified.
words.example: Accordingly, Mrs. Sharp turned the box out onto the table.
words.exampleText: Mrs. Sharp acted in accordance with what had just been said.

words.definition: Strange or unusual.
words.example: It was a queer collection of things—letters and lace and souvenirs.
words.exampleText: The collection was strange and unusual.

words.definition: Objects kept as a reminder of a place or event.
words.example: A queer collection of things—letters and lace and souvenirs.
words.exampleText: Small keepsakes from travels or events.

words.definition: Shriveled or shrunken withered by age or lack of moisture.
words.example: A withered nosegay his mother must have carried at the wedding.
words.exampleText: The small bouquet was dried up and shriveled.

words.definition: A small bunch of flowers.
words.example: A withered nosegay his mother must have carried at the wedding.
words.exampleText: Small floral bouquet carried or given as a gift.

words.definition: Made a rapid succession of short sharp noises; shook and made noise.
words.example: Mrs. Sharp’s hat rattled as she bent swiftly over these.
words.exampleText: The hat made a shaking noise as she moved.

words.definition: Quickly; rapidly.
words.example: Mrs. Sharp bent swiftly over these.
words.exampleText: She leaned over them very quickly.

words.definition: Earrings made with or set with diamonds.
words.example: Those are diamond earrings!
words.exampleText: Precious jeweled earrings featuring diamonds.

words.definition: A shop that sells secondhand articles and miscellaneous objects, often of little value.
words.example: Mr. Larkins kept the junk shop on the corner of the street.
words.exampleText: Store dealing with used or discarded items.

words.definition: Ornamental metal guards placed in front of fireplaces to protect the surrounding area.
words.example: Among the brass fenders and chipped crockery you could find quite valuable things.
words.exampleText: Copper or brass guards for hearths.

words.definition: Dishware that has small pieces broken off the edges.
words.example: Among the brass fenders and chipped crockery you could find quite valuable things.
words.exampleText: Damaged plates or cups with chips.

words.definition: A subtle or careful written announcement or sign.
words.example: A discreet notice saying Exotic Supplies.
words.exampleText: A quietly displayed sign indicating exotic goods.

words.definition: Rare or unusual goods or ingredients, often imported from foreign places.
words.example: A discreet notice saying Exotic Supplies.
words.exampleText: Uncommon materials or items from abroad.

words.definition: Substances used in magical spells or potions.
words.example: Bats’ wings, dried newts, and other ingredients of magic.
words.exampleText: Items needed for witchcraft or magic potions.

words.definition: Bulged or swollen, typically describing eyes.
words.example: Mrs. Sharp’s eyes pouched up, greedy and beady.
words.exampleText: Her eyes became prominent and glistening.

words.definition: Small, round, and shining, like beads, often describing eyes that are sharp or intense.
words.example: Mrs. Sharp’s eyes pouched up, greedy and beady.
words.exampleText: Eyes looking sharp and gleaming.

words.definition: Having an intense and selfish desire for something.
words.example: Mrs. Sharp’s eyes pouched up, greedy and beady.
words.exampleText: Showing extreme desire for possession.

words.definition: An intense but unseen contest or conflict.
words.example: There was a feeling of fierce invisible struggle as their hands paused in the air.
words.exampleText: A strong, unseen fight or competition.

words.definition: In an unemotional or unfriendly manner.
words.example: Thank you,” Gwendolen said coldly.
words.exampleText: Spoken without warmth or kindness.

words.definition: Genuine ability or skill in a particular area.
words.example: You have real talent, dearie!
words.exampleText: True skill or aptitude.

words.definition: A small bunch of flowers or foliage arranged together.
words.example: She picked up a spray of white heather.
words.exampleText: A small floral bouquet or cluster.

words.definition: A low-growing shrub with purple or white flowers.
words.example: A spray of white heather.
words.exampleText: Plant often found in moorlands.

words.definition: A collection of things wrapped or tied together.
words.example: She picked up a bundle of old letters.
words.exampleText: Several items grouped and fastened.

words.definition: Quickly moving fingers or thumb over something, such as pages or papers.
words.example: Mrs. Sharp’s broad mauve thumb whirring down a bundle of letters.
words.exampleText: Rapidly moving the thumb over the edges.

words.definition: Loosened or undone something that is tied.
words.example: She untied the pink tape from around the bundle.
words.exampleText: Removed the binding by undoing it.

words.definition: Spoke suddenly and loudly, often in surprise or strong emotion.
words.example: “Chrestomanci!” she exclaimed.
words.exampleText: Expressed an outburst of feeling verbally.

words.definition: Spoke quietly and unclearly, often with the mouth partly closed.
words.example: She clapped one hand over her mouth and mumbled behind it.
words.exampleText: Spoke indistinctly or in a low voice.

words.definition: Feeling or showing astonishment or shock.
words.example: She was surprised, frightened, and greedy, all at the same time.
words.exampleText: Reacting unexpectedly to something.
